turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument this morning in Case 12짯10882, Hall v. Florida. Mr. Waxman.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: In Atkins v. Virginia, this Court held that the Constitution bars executing persons with mental retardation; that is, persons with significantly subaverage intellectual function concurrent with deficits in adaptive behavior with an onset before the age of 18. Because of the standard error of measurement that's inherent in IQ tests, it is universally accepted that persons with obtained scores of 71 to 75 can and often do have mental retardation when those three prongs are met. The statistical error of measurement or SEM is that 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Waxman, a line has to be drawn somewhere. And we did say in Atkins that we would leave it up to the States to determine the standards for this issue. So what's the rule we announce today? We tell them 70 is not okay, but 75 would be? I'm not quite sure. How would you announce the rule?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Let me first take 짯짯 take some issue, with all due respect, with your characterization of Atkins. What this Court said in Atkins is not that we leave it to the States to establish the standards for the clinical condition of mental retardation. What you said, quoting Ford, is, "We leave it to the States to 짯짯 we leave to the States the task of developing appropriate ways to enforce the constitutional restriction that we announce." The rule that we advocate is 짯짯 and the only real question presented in this case is just this: If a State conditions the opportunity to demonstrate mental retardation on obtained IQ test scores, it cannot ignore the measurement error that is inherent in those scores that is a feature, statistical feature of the test instrument itself.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But 짯짯 but we didn't 짯짯 we didn't base our decision in Atkins upon a study of what the American Psychiatric Association and other medical associations considered to be mental retardation. We based it on what 짯짯 what was the general rule that States had adopted. And a large number of States had adopted 70 as the criterion. I mean, the criterion is what do the American people think is the level of mental retardation that should make it impossible to impose the death penalty. We didn't look for the answer to that question to the APA or any of the other medical associations. We looked to what the States did. Now, what has changed in what the States do? Anything?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Justice Scalia, I have 짯짯 I would like to respond with four points, and I hope desperately I'll remember them. First of all, what this Court said was, this Court 짯짯 number one, it made clear, as it has reiterated in Miller v. Alabama and Graham v. Florida, that while a consensus or a perceived consensus among the States is important, the ultimate test is this Court's conclusion about what the Eighth Amendment does or doesn't allow. In making that determination at page 318 of this Court's opinion in Atkins, this Court, after reciting in Footnote 3 the virtually identical clinical definitions of mental retardation, and in Footnote 5, pointing out that 70 to 75 is the established cutoff for mental retardation, this Court said the following: Quote, "Clinical definitions of mental retardation require" 짯짯 and it recited the three tests. "Because of their impairments, mentally retarded persons by definition" 짯짯 that is by the clinical definition 짯짯 "have diminished capacities to understand" 짯짯 and it recited all the other disabilities that made the imposition of the death penalty for persons with that 짯짯 excuse me 짯짯 with that clinical condition unconstitutional. Now, as to what the States did, the Court did refer to, I believe, 18 State statutes. Not a single one of those State statutes and not a single decision of the highest court of any State or any court in any State applied 70 or two standard deviations from the mean without reference to the SEM. The only statute that addressed it in 2002, when this Court decided Atkins, was Arizona, which expressly provided that the SEM must be taken into account in evaluating the 짯짯 an obtained IQ test score.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The SEM being 짯짯 being what and 짯짯 and established by whom?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: The standard error of measurement, which is established by the creators of the test. It is not something that clinicians dream up. It's not something that is decided by the AAIDD or the American Psychiatric Association. It is inherent in the test. And all clinicians are told 짯짯 both professional associations make clear, because it is simply a statistical fact, it must be taken into account such that an obtained IQ test score is actually the result of an obtained IQ test score, is a test band that accounts for the standard.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: For what purpose do they establish these scores? Is it for the purpose of determining who is so incapable of 짯짯 of controlling his actions that he shouldn't be subject to the death penalty? Is that 짯짯 is that what they're looking for when they establish 70 to 75? What are they looking for?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, what they're 짯짯 they are looking for 짯짯 I mean, intelligence tests supply a 짯짯 I mean, they weren't created for the definition of 짯짯 clinical definition of mental retardation. They were created as a 짯짯 in order to determine a proxy for true intellectual function. And therefore 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: 짯짯 a true IQ test score 짯짯 I mean, the general clinical 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm not talking about IQ tests in general. I'm talking about why do they pick 짯짯 they used to pick 70. Now they pick between 70 and 75 as the upper limit. What are 짯짯 upper limit for what? I assume it is for people who would profit from medical treatment. Isn't that it?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: There are many reasons why a person's IQ, that is, a person's intelligent 짯짯 intellectual functioning, may be important for a whole variety of reasons, medical, psychological, developmental, and as a component of the clinical condition of mental retardation, the Eighth Amendment. Now, what intellectual 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Waxman, could we just clarify one thing, that what you refer to as the SEM, that is not limited to IQ of 70, 75. That's across the board.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I mean, the 짯짯 the concept, the statistical concept of a standard error of measurement has 짯짯 applies to all forms of testing.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: So it has nothing to do with the death penalty and mental retardation 짯짯

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: No. I mean, I'm sure that, you know, when Archimedes announced his principle based on experimental 짯짯 his experimental observations, he also recognized the 짯짯 essentially the standard error of measurement.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: May I come back to a question? May I come back to something similar to what Justice Sotomayor started out with. In your view, does the Constitution establish a State to establish any hard cutoff? Let's say 76. Can it do that?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I think it can because that falls 짯짯 because the standard definition of prong one, that is, intellectual functioning, is two or more standard deviations below the mean.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: All right. If it can do that 짯짯 oh, I'm sorry.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I'm sorry. Let me just 짯짯 let me just explain. And because 짯짯 if 짯짯 if a State is using an obtained IQ test score as a proxy for true intellectual function, it has to take into account the standard error of measurement. And therefore, States like Mississippi and Oklahoma that, in fact, establish a cutoff of 75, in our view, is constitutional as this Court announced the class of individuals in Atkins because 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So that's just saying 짯짯 I'm sorry. When you say the standard error of measuring, you're talking about a degree of confidence, right?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: And your submission is that you need to have a 95 percent degree of confidence. That's what 짯짯 that's what the 5 gives you or do I have the numbers wrong?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I thought the standard 짯짯

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: The 짯짯 on a test that is normed at 100 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: 짯짯 70 is two standard deviations below the mean. If there is a 짯짯 the standard error of measurement 짯짯 and it's not 짯짯 this is not my submission. This is the universal 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I know. I'm just trying to figure out what it means.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: That's exactly 짯짯 what it means is that someone, for example, with an I 짯짯 an obtained IQ test score of 71, as Mr. Hall received, has a 95 percent probability 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: 짯짯 that his score will be between 76 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So why is 95 percent? Where does that come from?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: That 짯짯 that is 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: 짯짯 under Atkins? Why 짯짯 why are you picking 95 percent? Why isn't it 90 percent?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I'm not doing any picking.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Why did the other 짯짯 why did the 짯짯 the organizations pick 95 percent.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: It's been 95 짯짯 it's been two standard error of 짯짯 two SEMs, which is 95 percent, for decades and decades, and this Court recognized that consensus, that universal consensus, in footnote 5 in its opinion in 짯짯

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Which party has the burden of persuasion on the issue of IQ and what is the standard?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: So it varies from State to State.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I mean, what 짯짯 what does the Eighth Amendment require? Does the Eighth Amendment permit a State to assign to the defendant the burden of persuasion on 짯짯 on IQ, IQ above 75? Can they assign that burden of 짯짯 above 70? Can they assign that to the defendant, and if they can what is the standard of proof that the defendant has to meet?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: So I 짯짯 the short answer is, I believe, what I will come to is yes, so that you see where I'm going. But we believe that it is entirely constitutional for the State to assign the burden of proving mental retardation on the defendant. And insofar as the clinical definition recognized by this Court in Atkins is a three짯part conjunctive test, I think it's fair to say that a logical consequence of that is that as to every component the burden may constitutionally be placed on the defendant. Now, the burden with respect to prong one is the burden of proving significantly subaverage intellectual functioning, of which a true IQ score is a probabilistic piece of evidence. I don't think 짯짯

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Why can't the State 짯짯 you told me that the State can establish a hard cutoff. And you told me that a State can assign the burden to the defendant. Now, in the case of someone who scores 75, is it not the case that there's roughly 짯짯 there's no more than a 2.5 percent chance that that person's real IQ is 70. So how does that square with any burden of proof that might be 짯짯 any standard of proof that might be assigned on that 짯짯 on that point? That's what I don't understand about your argument.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I think 짯짯 let me see if I can explain this. First of all, we're talking 짯짯 I mean, this is a man who has a 71.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: No, I understand.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: But I'm talking about the general issue. Just hypothetically 짯짯

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: As 짯짯 as to the general issue, let me 짯짯 let me 짯짯 let me state it this way: The whole idea behind measurement error is that you can't make a valid judgment that somebody doesn't have a true score of 70 or below if the obtained score is within the measurement error. And even more fundamental than that, the 짯짯 your question suggests and the State's suggestion suggests that diagnosing mental retardation, which is the constitutional inquiry, is just a probabilistic inquiry into a person's, quote, "true" IQ score. But true IQ scores themselves are a statistical concept. It's the score that you would get on a hypothetical test that had no measurement error. But true 짯짯 and this is my point 짯짯 true IQ is not the same as intellectual function and IQ tests themselves, however perfect they may be, don't perfectly capture a person's intellectual function, which is why 짯짯

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I understand that argument. But that doesn't seem to me consistent with your point that a State can establish a hard cutoff. 76, that's the end. You get a 76 on an IQ test, that's the end of the inquiry. The person does not 짯짯 does not 짯짯 does not qualify under Atkins.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: So I 짯짯 what I'm 짯짯 this 짯짯 this would not be a standard I would endorse, but I believe that in light of the consensus test that all professional organizations apply that was recognized in Atkins, a score that is above the standard error of measurement of two standard deviations above the mean would be okay. But the point 짯짯 the converse point it seems to me is not true, which is we know for a fact that many, many people who obtain test scores of 71 to 75, in fact, have mental retardation. And if I just may point out that in this case, there were six experts who fully examined Mr. Hall or supervised a full examination of Mr. Hall. They were cognizant of the IQ test scores that he had received. And each one of them opined without hesitation that he had mental retardation, functional mental retardation, significant 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Retrospectively.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Excuse me?

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: The district court did make a finding that he did not show adaptive behavior, and the district court said that that was so because all of those experts that you've referred to were speaking retrospectively. There was no evidence of what the defendant's current condition was. That was 짯짯 I think it's in the Joint Appendix 짯짯

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: That is correct, Justice Ginsburg. Now, the State trial court ruled that it would not accept evidence as to prongs two and three, but it did allow Mr. Hall's lawyers to make a proffer pursuant to the State's 짯짯 the State's agreement that there could be a proffer in some expeditious manner. And that's at Joint Appendix 158. We 짯짯 one of the two grounds that we appealed to the Florida Supreme Court on, in addition to the hard cutoff at 70, was the fact that in fact an expeditious proffer did not in fact permit us to put on all of our evidence about prongs two and three. And the Florida Supreme Court, and this is page 125 of the Joint Appendix, said: We don't need to consider that question because we uphold the rule in Cherry.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, what 짯짯 there was nothing that limited you to the retrospective proof. The 짯짯 the trial judge asked a simple question, how did the defendant adapt in prison, and quotes one expert as saying: "Well, I didn't test for that. I don't know why I didn't do it." And that same expert said that he had, in fact, done it in other cases.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: You're correct. Part of the expeditious proffer 짯짯 the expeditious proffer was limited to the testimony of two of 짯짯 I believe actually only one of the experts who examined him and did the adaptive testing function, and that expert did say that he didn't test in prison. Now, as 짯짯 there is, again, a universal professional consensus that adaptive functioning is tested by adaptive functioning in the real world, not adaptive functioning that occurs on 짯짯 after 35 years on death row. And, in fact, we also know to a clinical certainty that because mental retardation is a condition that is both developmental and not transient, that is, there has to be an onset 짯짯 demonstrated onset during the developmental period, but one doesn't emerge from the condition of mental retardation, unlike, for example, mental illness.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: If you talk about the condition of mental disability that's involved here, I want to go back to something you've said in response to Justice Scalia. The question was along the line of what does it mean to have a disorder under the DSM. Obviously, one thing it means is that the scholars can talk about it; that they can all focus on the same subject. Does it have any meaning other than that, that it 짯짯 it is an objective index, an objective characterization that certain people have a certain mental condition? Is 짯짯 is that what it means?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: That's exactly what it means, Justice Kennedy. What it means is, it is a 짯짯 as this Court recognized, it is a clinical condition, unlike, for example, insanity or competence. That the clinical 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is 짯짯 is there 짯짯 is there any evidence that society in general gives substantial deference to the psychiatric profession in this respect? Are there any studies on that or is there anything we can look to to see that that's true on not true?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I 짯짯 I'm actually not aware of anything that suggests that one 짯짯 that society doesn't look to professional evaluations to do this. And, in fact, if one looks only at Florida's system, Florida uses mental retardation as a determinant for things other than the death penalty. It uses the existence of the condition for educational remediation, vocational rehabilitation and everything. And in those instances, as we point out in our brief, the 짯짯 the Florida 짯짯 Florida does apply the standard error of measurement. And indeed 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: We have later in the week an argument about economic theories. And it's a little different because in that case, the Court 짯짯 it's the Court's own jurisprudence and we have not said, as we have in Atkins, that it's up to the State. But do you think we defer to psychiatric 짯짯 psychologists and psychiatrists any more than we 짯짯 or any less than we do to economists?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Oh, I think it has to be much, much, more because, as this Court pointed out, this is a clinical condition. It's a condition that can only be appropriately diagnosed by professionals.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mister 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: They change 짯짯 they changed their mind, counsel. This APA is the same organization that once said that homosexuality was a 짯짯 was a mental disability and now says it's perfectly normal. They change their minds.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Justice Scalia 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And they have changed their minds as to whether 70 or 75 is the 짯짯 is the new test 짯짯 for for mental retardation.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: The latter is not true. The 짯짯 the standard 짯짯 two things that are not in dispute in this case. We're only here talking about prong one, which is significantly subaverage intellectual functioning and nothing else. And everyone agrees, all the States agree, they all agreed at the time Atkins was decided, that their 짯짯 that the clinical definition is defined by three elements and that the first element, significantly subaverage intellectual functioning is defined as a person whose intellectual function is two or more standard deviations below the mean intellectual functioning of contemporary society.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Waxman, can I take you back to a question that the Chief Justice asked? Because the Chief Justice said, you know, where does this SEM come from. And it is the test maker's determination that this is the margin of error that gives you a 95 percent confidence. I guess the question here or one question here is why do we have a 95 percent 짯짯 why do we need a 95 percent confidence level? And you could say it either way. You could say, gosh, we're putting somebody to death, we should 짯짯 we should have a 100 percent confidence level. Or you could say, as I take it Justice Alito 짯짯 Justice Alito's point was, well, look, the burden of proof is on the defendant here anyway, so a 95 percent confidence level seems awfully high. We should, you know, ration it down to 80 percent. So why for this purpose do we have to go with the test maker's determination that 5 is what gives you a 95 percent confidence level?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: So the fact that two SEMs gives you a 95 percent confidence level is just the statistical fact. I take your question to be, well, why does 짯짯 you know, why do clinicians and professional associations use that?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well 짯짯 and that's not really my question.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Oh, I see.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I understand why they might use it for a wide variety of purposes. The question is: Why does their determination that it's useful for a wide variety of purposes to have a 95 percent confidence level, why is the State stuck with that for this purpose?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Because the whole 짯짯 and this goes to the reason that they use it. The reason that they use it is because of the inherent imprecision in testing in general, but in particular testing for the presence of something like relative intellectual functioning. There are so many 짯짯 it is so common for people who, for a variety of reasons, obtain a 71 or 72, in fact, to have mental retardation and because evidence of 짯짯 an evaluation of intellectual function involves clinically much more than a test score. I mean, look what happened in this case. All of the IQ tests that were administered, all of the Wechsler tests, were accompanied, because they fell within the standard error of measurement, they were accompanied by the administration of further intelligence testing for confirmatory purposes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Is that what you want? That is, I go back to Justice Sotomayor's question. Start monkeying around with 95 percent. It's all over the law. I mean, 95 percent is a classical measure by scientists of when they have confidence that the fact that the regression analysis seems to establish is in fact a fact.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That is in tort law. That is in whether jury trials are 짯짯 are discriminating because they don't have black people on the jury. It's all over the law. So I assume that we 짯짯 you're not asking us to muck around with that number because I don't know what the consequences would be. And if you're not, here's how we reduce it. You give the same test six times and now we've reduced it from 5 percent, if he's above 70 all the time, to maybe one 짯짯 one one짯hundredth of one percent. Is that what you want to have happen?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, let me just, as to your latter point 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Am I right? Am I right in what I said?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: You are not right in some of the things you've said.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: The last thing you said is not right, which is 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, no. Let's go before the last thing.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, the last thing is important.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I'm not saying it isn't important, but I want 짯짯 my thinking to the last thing is dependent on my being right on everything before the last thing. So am I right before the last thing, about how 95 percent 짯짯

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: You are right that 95 percent is just a feature 짯짯 is, generally speaking, a feature that is widely adopted as a confidence level, and it is particularly important here because the constitutional guarantee announced in Atkins is against the execution of persons with mental retardation. And 짯짯

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: On Justice Breyer's last point, before your 짯짯 your time expires, because I do think this is important. Is there not another way of proving reliability? Suppose 짯짯 what about multiple tests? Suppose someone is given 25 Wechsler tests and 24 times the person scores 76 and one time the person scores 72. What would you deal with 짯짯 how would you deal with that in a State that has a hard cutoff?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: So, this is the last point that I wanted to get to, and I think if you 짯짯 that the best thing I can 짯짯 before my time runs out, I just want to point you to page 10, Footnote 3 of our reply brief, which cites the Oxford Handbook of, I don't know, Clinical Diagnosis or something, and we've given you the pages. And on those pages, it explains why when you have a situation of somebody who takes more than one test, the appropriate determinant is very much not the average. It is what's called the composite score. And the composite score is different, and, in fact, for people below the mean, below the average, because you have to take into account the fact that regression towards the mean and also, the fact that a person who takes two, three, or four tests, multiple tests, changes the bell curve of standard deviation. So the example that's given in the Oxford Handbook is very similar to this case. There were four tests. They averaged at 72. The composite score, and there's 짯짯 there's a 짯짯 there's a statistical explanation for how it's arrived at. The composite score is 69, and the standard error of measurement is actually larger using a composite score. So that's why, as to Justice Breyer's last point, simply averaging obtained scores does not, in fact, give you a better handle. Because there are so few people who score significantly below the mean on multiple tests, what clinicians use is a statistical analysis that takes into account the different 짯짯 the different calculation of what a standard deviation below the mean is.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: That's not consistent with my understanding of it, but I don't claim that I have a deep understanding of it. But what 짯짯 what would be your answer to my hypothetical? Where there are multiple scores that are above the hard cutoff but one that's below 짯짯 and I will ask the State the opposite question 짯짯 what would you do there?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, we know what Florida does, which 짯짯

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, what does the Eighth Amendment require, in your view?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, in our view, the Eighth Amendment requires that if a State chooses to use IQ test scores as a proxy for intellectual functioning rather than a full inquiry into intellectual functioning, it cannot refuse to employ the standard error of measurement that is inherent in the test.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: And if it were 76, you would not need to go on to adaptive behavior; is that your view?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Our 짯짯 our view is that a State consistent with Atkins could say that if you have no obtained score on a valid, properly administered, up짯to짯date test that is 70 짯짯 that is below 76, you may 짯짯 you may constitutionally be precluded. I think many clinicians would go ahead and do adaptive functioning and other intellectual functioning. But our view is that States like Mississippi and Oklahoma that set 76 as the cutoff do, in fact, comply with Atkins. May I save the balance of my time.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This Court should affirm the decision of the Florida Supreme Court because it represents a reasonable legislative judgment and one that is fully consistent with Atkins and the Eighth Amendment. I would like to start by responding to your question, Justice Alito, about what 짯짯 what do you do with multiple scores. And in fact in this case we're not talking about someone who had one or two IQ scores. When you look at the Wechsler test, which is what the Petitioner contends is the gold standard, he had test scores of 71, 72, 73, 74 and 80. And as we understand, what the Petitioner would have this Court do is to take some of those lower scores and simply subtract 5 points from them. That is not consistent with 짯짯 with the materials that he cited in the footnote in his brief. If you look at the example there, they 짯짯 they do apply some statistical principles to a range of scores, but they do not simply take the lowest score and subtract 5 points from it. And the logic of that, I would submit, is fairly obvious. You couldn't have a situation where, take in this case, you have an 짯짯 a low IQ on the Wechsler of 71 and a high IQ on the Wechsler of 80, and say at the same time that there is a 95 percent chance his score is between 75 and 85 and also a 95 percent chance that his score is between 66 and 76.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA

turns.text: Well, that's just to say that it would double the number of people eligible, but some of them may be mentally retarded. I mean, presumably we want accurate decisionmaking with respect to this question, don't we?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, there 짯짯 there 짯짯 we do certainly. And they are not mentally retarded if they don't have an IQ of 70 or below. And that's a 짯짯 that's a position that Petitioner doesn't 짯짯 doesn't challenge.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Who are not mentally retarded if they don't have an IQ score of 70 or below? I mean, you 짯짯 you don't believe that yourself, right? This is a tool to decide whether somebody is mentally retarded, and it's a tool that functions in one prong of a three짯prong test.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: It is the first prong. The IQ threshold is the first prong. So 짯짯 no matter what your adaptive deficits are, you must demonstrate 짯짯 and, again, here in this adversary setting, you must demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that you have an IQ of 70 or below. And what we believe is that if you say, Well, there is a 95 percent chance that my IQ is somewhere between, say, 68 and 78, that you have not satisfied that first prong. And I would like to talk about the 95 percent confidence interval, because it is not the case that you have, say, with a 72 a 95 percent chance that your IQ is 70 or below. In fact, it's a very small chance. What the 짯짯 what the confidence interval measures is that you have a 95 percent chance that your true IQ is within five points of the measured thing, the measured IQ, but it's not that you would have an equal chance of having a 66, a 67, a 68. It falls under the bell curve. And so if you take the test over and over again, you are going to score near the 짯짯 near the peak of that bell curve most of the time, which is where your true IQ would be. And at the outer ends of that 95 percent threshold are very, very small likelihoods that you 짯짯 that that's your true IQ. And 짯짯 and then with each additional test you take, the odds 짯짯 that 짯짯 that's above 70, the odds would go down. And so it's simply not the case that you can say, Well, he has a 72 so he has satisfied or even might have satisfied the first prong because, as a statistical matter, every 짯짯 well, as a factual matter, every Wechsler test he has taken that was admitted into evidence was over 70. He had a 71, a 72, 73, 74, and an 80. And so if you want to apply statistics to it, you would have to look and say, well, what are the 짯짯 what are the odds that with that group of testing, that his true IQ is under 70? Now, is it possible? Certainly it is possible that it's over 100. You know, you can exceed beyond the 95 percent confidence interval. And nobody disputes that 짯짯 that the true IQ is something that is incapable of being measured or incapable of 짯짯 and 짯짯 but 짯짯 but the IQ test is what the community has, and it's the most objective of the three prongs, which is why we believe it's particularly important to focus on because it's the most objective test that we have.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: How many States retain that practice with a rigid 70 cutoff?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Your Honor, by our count there are eight States that have both a hard cutoff and a 짯짯 and a 70 or two standard deviations, which approximates to the same thing, that has been expressly recognized by the States. There are a number of other States that have statutes similar to Florida's, but that have not been interpreted one way or the other that 짯짯 that we may or may not 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Of those eight, how many actually have a fixed cutoff and how many have a SEM? I thought it was only four that didn't have consideration.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: No, no. Those eight, Your Honor, all have a fixed cutoff of 70 or two standard 짯짯 two standard deviations.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But by judicial decision, they've considered 짯짯 well, we 짯짯 that 짯짯 that's something that 짯짯

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: In 짯짯 in most of the instances, Your Honor, they have done what Florida has done, which is they've had a statute that then was interpreted by the 짯짯 by the courts. So we 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Exactly. That's what I'm saying. Only four have it interpreted without the SEM.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm 짯짯 I apologize.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I thought only four had interpreted without using the SEM?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Had interpreted their statutes without using the SEM?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Only four, like Florida.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, we have eight. We have Alabama, Florida, Idaho, Kansas, Kentucky, North Carolina, and Virginia, and Maryland, which has repealed the death penalty, but 짯짯 but that was their standard when they have it. We would ask respectfully that the Court affirm the Florida Supreme Court.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, General. Mr. Waxman, you have a minute remaining.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: In State v. Cherry, which is the 짯짯 the Florida Supreme Court decision that established this rule that if you 짯짯 if your lowest score or your only score is 71, you are out, and that applies whether you take one test or multiple tests 짯짯 here I'm quoting from the Supreme Court's decision in Cherry, that, quote, "It is a universally accepted given, that is that the SEM is a universally accepted given, and as such should logically be considered in determining whether a defendant has mental retardation." What the Court said was: We have to read the plain meaning of the Florida statute and the Florida statute says two standard deviations. The notion that the Florida legislature or the 짯짯 may I finish my sentence 짯짯 the Florida legislature or the people of Florida have made a considered decision not to account for the SEM is baseless and is belied by the 짯짯 the legislative report that accompanies the statute which said 70 to 75. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Waxman, counsel. The case is submitted.
